summary:
The Han-Xiongnu Wars (200–89 BC) were a prolonged series of military conflicts between the [[event:77|Han Dynasty]] and the [[event:1133|Xiongnu Empire]], the powerful confederation of nomadic peoples who dominated the Mongolian steppe and threatened China's northern frontier — a struggle that shaped Chinese foreign policy, drove the opening of the [[event:1129|Silk Road]], and established the strategic framework of Chinese relations with the northern steppe for centuries.

The conflict began badly for Han: Emperor Gaozu was surrounded by Xiongnu forces at the Battle of Baideng (200 BC) and only escaped through a humiliating negotiation, after which Han adopted the heqin policy of purchasing peace through tribute payments and royal marriages. This policy of appeasement continued for decades until [[event:1127|Emperor Wu of Han]] reversed course with an aggressive military strategy. Employing large cavalry armies and new tactics, Han generals including Wei Qing and Huo Qubing drove the Xiongnu from the Ordos region and Gansu corridor in the 120s BC, opening the routes to Central Asia. Later campaigns pushed deep into the Mongolian steppe. The Xiongnu were weakened but not destroyed — they eventually split into northern and southern factions, with the southern Xiongnu accepting Han suzerainty while the northern group migrated westward, eventually contributing to the migrations that destabilized the Roman Empire centuries later.
